design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling stages, manage power gauge for weapon strength timing, defeat enemies with axe/spear/sword, collect power-ups to progress
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward between rapid weak attacks versus slower powerful swings; weapon choice trade-offs (damage vs. magic efficiency)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Teenage hero summoned to a magical kingdom, wielding a legendary axe to rescue a princess and confront an evil doppelgänger
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Weapon progression system that penalizes player choice—selecting a stronger sword makes magic spells more expensive, forcing strategic commitment to playstyle. Auto-filling power gauge creates deliberate pacing rhythm rather than constant input. Narrative divergence from arcade (school student in magical world vs. mythological warrior) suggests NES port was conceptually reimagined for younger audience.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives with health gauge per life; revert to previous weapon tier upon death
difficulty.continues_system: Arcade version allows continues at any moment; NES version supports continues but specifics not documented
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; no mid-stage checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Weapon selection provides agency—players can choose between faster weak attacks (spear) or slower strong ones (sword), directly affecting magic cost efficiency and combat rhythm

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon evolution chain: Axe → Spear → Sword (NES version); power gauge extensions via collectible power-ups; magic gauge management through weapon selection and consumable refills
progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential stage completion (forest → cave → lake → lift → castle → final battle); weapon upgrades obtained by destroying pillars or encountering Cutie NPC for weapon/magic refill exchanges
replayability_hooks: Different weapon choices create distinct playstyles and magic consumption patterns
Power-up management and collection optimization on replay
Arcade version's two-player simultaneous co-op mode

world_building_techniques: NES-specific narrative reframe: contemporary high school setting (Greenview High School) contrasts with fantasy realm (Remlia), suggesting inter-dimensional portal concept
Dream motif as story anchor—protagonist's recurring premonitions justify summoning and establish emotional investment
NPC companion (Cutie the fairy) provides exposition and sacrificial story beat, creating emotional stakes
Twist revelation: Blackhorn as Astyanax's evil alternate self, literalizing internal conflict as external boss encounter
Reward structure: Cutie's resurrection as human signals narrative closure and relationship payoff

boss_design.philosophy: Final boss (Blackhorn) functions as psychological mirror/alternate identity rather than purely external threat. Pattern-based combat with learnable tells implicit in power gauge management system.
notable_bosses: Blackhorn (final boss): Evil alternate version of protagonist; serves dual mechanical and narrative role
Argos (arcade version final boss): Demon overlord; contextualizes journey as mythological quest

faction_indicators: Protagonist alignment: young hero summoned by kingdom (good faction)
Princess Rosebud's capture establishes princess/kingdom faction in distress
Blackhorn as corrupting force/evil alternate self represents internal darkness made manifest
danger_signals: Demonic creatures throughout stages (arcade frame: world ruled by demons)
Evil wizard Blackhorn controls kingdom and holds princess captive
Trap mechanism in final confrontation indicates deception and danger escalation
environmental_storytelling: Stage sequence (forest → cave → lake → lift → castle) implies geographical/structural progression toward wizard's seat of power
Mythical creature population in arcade version establishes high-fantasy tone versus school-to-other-world contrast in NES
Cutie's sacrifice location (Blackhorn's castle) marks moral turning point and isolation of protagonist for final challenge
